title: Analytical Study of Dual-Porosity Coastal Aquifer with Generalized Nonlinear Water Transfer Term

research.question: How does a generalized nonlinear matrix-to-fracture transfer term change Earth-tide head propagation and intrusion distance in a dual-porosity coastal aquifer?
research.system: A one-dimensional semi-infinite fractured coastal aquifer in which fractures transmit groundwater and lower-permeability matrix blocks store and exchange water.
research.inputData: Fracture transmissivity and storage, matrix storage, transfer coefficient, generalized-transfer weight, tidal amplitude and frequency, and coastal and inland boundary conditions.
research.method: Couple fracture and lumped-matrix balances through a generalized transfer term, obtain harmonic solutions by direct Fourier analysis, solve the spatial coefficients, and evaluate amplitudes, intrusion distance, and parameter sensitivity.
research.benchmark: The generalized model is reduced to and compared with the existing first-order Jacob-type analytical response under the corresponding limiting transfer condition.
research.uncertainty: Normalized local sensitivity identifies influential parameters; conclusions are conditional on a one-dimensional homogeneous dual-porosity representation and the generalized transfer closure.
research.conclusion: Increasing the generalized-transfer weight increases fracture-head amplitude and landward intrusion while changing matrix-head amplitude in a distance-dependent manner.

narrative.throughline: Track an ocean-tide pressure wave into a fractured aquifer, then watch nonlinear exchange with matrix storage redistribute amplitude between the two continua.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: A coastal tidal wave attenuates landward through aquifer transmissivity and storage.
narrative.stakes: In fractured media, treating the aquifer as one continuum or fixing matrix transfer to one order can misstate the fracture response that controls intrusion.
narrative.resolution: A weighted generalized transfer term continuously bridges first- and second-order exchange and makes its effect on each porosity and intrusion distance explicit.

background.premise: The paper represents a homogeneous one-dimensional fractured coastal aquifer with coupled fracture and matrix continua.
background.visibleEvidence: A tidal crest propagates landward along a one-dimensional fracture continuum beside uniformly storing matrix blocks.
background.whyNow: Coastal management and saltwater-intrusion assessment require an interpretable link between tides and fractured-aquifer response.
background.boundary: The formulation is a homogeneous semi-infinite one-dimensional dual-porosity model rather than a mapped field fracture network.
background.locator: PDF page 2, Section 2.1 Model setup, Figure 1
background.sourceExcerpt: Figure 1 shows the schematic diagram of the fractured aquifer near the ocean.
background.motionPurpose: Show the prescribed coastal tide entering the fracture continuum and exchanging with matrix blocks.
background.stepDetail: Place the ocean boundary at x equals zero and propagate one sinusoidal crest landward along the fracture strip.
background.transitionBridge: The adjacent matrix blocks supply the secondary storage premise in B02.

background.premise: The matrix block acts as the secondary porosity that stores water in the dual-porosity concept.
background.visibleEvidence: Low-permeability blocks swell and drain beside rapidly responding fractures.
background.whyNow: Matrix storage changes how much tidal water remains available to the fracture continuum.
background.boundary: Matrix head is treated as spatially uniform within the lumped-parameter approximation.
background.sourceExcerpt: the matrix block serves as the secondary porosity to store the
background.motionPurpose: Separate rapid fracture conveyance from delayed block storage.
background.stepDetail: Fill matrix blocks as adjacent fracture head rises and drain them as it falls.
background.transitionBridge: Exchange between these continua requires a transfer law flexible enough for matrix geometry.

background.premise: A generalized transfer weight can move matrix-to-fracture flux from first-order to second-order behavior.
background.visibleEvidence: A transfer-law control slides continuously between linear and quadratic response curves.
background.whyNow: One fixed transfer order cannot represent all matrix-flow geometries described by the generalized model.
background.boundary: The weight is a model closure parameter ranging from zero to one in the paper's formulation.
background.sourceExcerpt: a weight factor that allows the MTF term to transition from the first-
background.motionPurpose: Morph the exchange relation between its two limiting orders.
background.stepDetail: Move lambda from zero to one while bending the transfer curve.
background.transitionBridge: The weighted law is inserted between fracture and matrix balances.

methods.motionPurpose: Balance longitudinal fracture flow against storage and matrix exchange.
methods.stepDetail: Place transmissive flux arrows along the fracture and exchange arrows into adjacent blocks.
methods.transitionBridge: The opposite side of each exchange arrow enters the matrix balance.

methods.input: Matrix storage and matrix-to-fracture transfer flux
methods.validity: Low matrix permeability supports the assumed spatially uniform head within each matrix block.

methods.motionPurpose: Collapse internal matrix variation to one storage level.
methods.stepDetail: Flatten each matrix block's internal head shading to a single value.
methods.transitionBridge: A shared transfer term couples the two balances.

methods.input: Fracture head, matrix head, transfer coefficient, and weight lambda

methods.motionPurpose: Change exchange magnitude as the generalized-transfer weight moves.

return.visualObject: Original fractured coastal-aquifer transect with paired fracture and matrix amplitude envelopes and the 0.25 percent intrusion marker
return.claim: Within the generalized dual-porosity model, a larger transfer weight increases fracture-flow amplitude while lowering matrix-flow amplitude within the reported near-coastal distance range.
return.claimBoundary: The inference is model-based for a homogeneous one-dimensional fractured coastal aquifer; the matrix trend is explicitly distance-dependent and the paper reports the decrease before 0.1 km.
return.sourceExcerpt: Our results show that an increase in the weight factor, λ, will result in a larger amplitude of the fracture flow, while an increase in Kuo-Chen Ma https://orcid.org/0000-0001-7883-3545 that leads to a lower amplitude of the matrix flow before 0.1 km

visual.accentObject: Matrix blocks exchanging water with a transmitting fracture
visual.geometryNotes: Use a coast-to-inland transect with a continuous fracture corridor and repeated lumped matrix blocks; convert tidal waves to paired phasors, then to amplitude envelopes and a threshold-defined intrusion distance.
